content: I'm trying to decide between a new laptop and a tablet for my work. I'm a veterinarian at a rural practice, so I need something portable but powerful enough to handle medical records and imaging software.
What kind of software do you use for your practice? That would help determine the minimum specs you'd need.
Mostly Avimark for records and some diagnostic imaging software. I've been using a bulky Dell that's five years old now, and my knee injury makes carrying it around painful.
I see. A lighter device would definitely help with your mobility. Have you considered a Surface Pro or something similar that could work as both a laptop and tablet?
I have, but I'm not sure about the keyboard. I spend hours typing up notes after farm visits. Also, I need something that can connect to external monitors when I'm at the office.
The Surface Pro keyboards are decent, though maybe not as comfortable for long typing sessions. What about a MacBook Air? They're lightweight and have good battery life.
I've been a Windows user forever, so switching would be a learning curve. My daughter keeps telling me to get an iPad with a keyboard case, but I'm skeptical about running my practice software on it.
That's a valid concern. Many veterinary software companies are developing iPad apps now, but compatibility varies. What's your budget range?

I was hoping to stay under $1500. I've been knitting more since my injury - it's oddly therapeutic - and I've been listening to tech podcasts while I work on projects. One host mentioned the new Lenovo ThinkPad X1 Carbon as being really durable.

The X1 Carbon is an excellent choice - very durable and lightweight. It would handle your software well and should be within your budget. Plus it has great keyboard feedback for all that typing you do after farm visits.
